label: Tenochtitlan (Aztec Capital)
sub: Capital of the Aztec Triple Alliance, founded 1325 on an island in Lake Texcoco. Population 200,000–300,000 at its peak — one of the world's largest cities. Destroyed and rebuilt as Mexico City after the 1521 Spanish conquest.

label: Cusco (Inca Capital)
sub: Sacred capital of Tawantinsuyu (the Inca Empire), rebuilt by Pachacuti into an imperial city shaped like a puma. Center of a road network spanning 40,000 km. Seized by Pizarro in November 1533.

label: Cajamarca (Atahualpa Captured)
sub: Site of the decisive ambush on November 16, 1532, where Pizarro's 168 men captured Inca Emperor Atahualpa, killing thousands of unarmed attendants. Atahualpa was held here for eight months before his execution.

label: Machu Picchu (Inca Royal Estate)
sub: High-altitude Inca estate built by Pachacuti c.1450, 2,430 meters above sea level. Never discovered by the Spanish, it was abandoned and forgotten until 1911. Now recognized as one of the finest examples of Inca engineering.

label: Tlaxcala (Spanish Alliance)
sub: Capital of the Tlaxcalan confederation, lifelong enemy of the Aztec empire. After initial battle, Tlaxcalans allied with Cortés in September 1519, providing the bulk of the military force that conquered Tenochtitlan. Rewarded with tax exemptions for centuries.

label: Cholula (Massacre Site, Oct 1519)
sub: Second-largest city in Mexico and major religious center, site of the Cholula massacre (October 1519) where Cortés and Tlaxcalan allies killed an estimated 3,000–6,000 unarmed Cholulans in the main plaza. The Great Pyramid of Cholula, the largest pyramid by volume in the world, stands here.

label: Teotihuacan
sub: Pre-Aztec metropolis at its peak c.450 CE with 100,000–200,000 inhabitants, then the largest city in the Americas. The Aztecs revered it as the birthplace of the gods and drew on its symbolism. The Pyramid of the Sun and Pyramid of the Moon dominate the site.

label: Chichén Itzá (Maya Center)
sub: Major Maya political and ceremonial center in the Yucatán Peninsula, at its height 750–1200 CE. Features El Castillo pyramid (Temple of Kukulcán), the Great Ball Court, and the Sacred Cenote. Partially occupied at time of Spanish arrival in Yucatán (1517).

label: Tikal (Classic Maya City)
sub: Major Classic Maya city in present-day Guatemala, with a peak population of 100,000–200,000 during 200–900 CE. Center of Classic Maya civilization before its collapse around 900 CE. The Spanish conquest of the Petén region didn't occur until 1697.

label: Copán (Maya Kingdom)
sub: Major Classic Maya site in present-day Honduras, known for its elaborate sculptures and hieroglyphic stairway. At its peak in the 7th–8th centuries CE under rulers like '18 Rabbit.' Conquered by Spain in the 1530s under Pedro de Alvarado.

label: Vilcabamba (Last Inca State)
sub: Remote jungle retreat where Manco Inca established the last independent Inca state after the failed siege of Cusco (1537). Vilcabamba resisted Spanish conquest for 35 years under three successive Sapa Incas until Viceroy Toledo's invasion in 1572.

label: Quito (Northern Inca Center)
sub: Northern administrative capital of the Inca empire, base of Atahualpa's power during the civil war with Huascar. Conquered by Sebastián de Benalcázar in 1534. Founded as a Spanish city on December 6, 1534.

label: Tula (Toltec Capital)
sub: Capital of the Toltec empire c.900–1150 CE. Venerated by the Aztecs as the legendary Tollan of their god-king Quetzalcóatl. Toltec warriors (Atlantes) and feathered-serpent imagery spread across Mesoamerica from here.

label: Monte Albán (Zapotec Capital)
sub: Major hilltop city of the Zapotec civilization in the Valley of Oaxaca, occupied from c.500 BCE to 700 CE. One of Mesoamerica's first cities. The Zapotec and Mixtec peoples of Oaxaca were incorporated into the Aztec tributary system before the Spanish arrival.

label: Uxmal (Puuc Maya)
sub: Major Maya city in the Yucatán known for its elaborate Puuc-style architecture. Peak occupation c.700–1000 CE. Located near Mérida (founded 1542), which was built on or near Maya settlement T'hó after Montejo conquered the Yucatan.

label: Mapuche Territory (Araucanía)
sub: Homeland of the Mapuche people south of the Biobío River in Chile, who successfully resisted Inca conquest before repelling Spanish invasion in the Araucanian (Arauco) War beginning 1550. The Mapuche maintained their independence until Chilean government military campaigns in the 1880s.

label: Palos de la Frontera (Columbus Departure)
sub: Spanish port from which Columbus departed on August 3, 1492, with the Niña, Pinta, and Santa María. The ships and crew were largely Andalusian. Crew members included Martin Alonso Pinzón (captain of the Pinta) and his brother Vicente Yáñez Pinzón.

label: Seville (Casa de Contratación)
sub: Spain's main colonial port and home of the Casa de Contratación (House of Trade, est. 1503), which monopolized all trade with the Americas. Seville controlled all legal commerce, immigration, and navigation to the Indies until 1717 when the monopoly transferred to Cádiz.

label: San Salvador (Columbus's First Landfall)
sub: The island Columbus named San Salvador on October 12, 1492, likely present-day Watlings Island in the Bahamas. Home of the Lucayan Taíno people, who welcomed Columbus. Within a generation, the entire Lucayan population was enslaved and transported, effectively exterminating the island's indigenous people.

label: La Navidad (First European Settlement)
sub: Fort built from the wreck of the Santa María on Christmas Day 1492 — the first European settlement in the Americas. Columbus left 39 men; all were killed by 1493, reportedly after abusing local Taíno. Columbus found the fort burned on his return in November 1493.

label: Veracruz (Cortés Landing, 1519)
sub: Gulf Coast site where Hernán Cortés founded Villa Rica de la Vera Cruz in April 1519, establishing the legal basis for his expedition independent of Governor Velázquez. The site became the primary port of New Spain and the entry point for goods, settlers, and enslaved Africans from Spain.

label: Darién (Balboa's Base)
sub: Settlement of Santa María la Antigua del Darién in present-day Colombia, first permanent European settlement on the American mainland (1510). Base from which Balboa crossed Panama to the Pacific in 1513. Governed brutally until replaced by Panama City (1519) as the regional capital.

label: Panama City (Pacific Gateway)
sub: Founded by Pedrarias Dávila in 1519, the first European city on the Pacific coast of the Americas. Strategic gateway for expeditions to Peru — all of Pizarro's South American ventures launched from here. Major terminus of the Camino Real (Royal Road) connecting the Pacific to the Atlantic.

label: Valladolid (Debate Site, 1550)
sub: Royal court city in Spain where the famous Valladolid Debate (1550–51) took place between Las Casas and Sepúlveda on the justness of the conquest and the humanity of indigenous people. Also where Columbus died in 1506 and where Ferdinand and Isabella maintained their court.

label: La Noche Triste (Tlacopan Causeway)
sub: Site on the western causeway of Lake Texcoco where the Spanish and their allies suffered catastrophic losses retreating from Tenochtitlan on the night of June 30–July 1, 1520. Over 450 Spaniards and thousands of Tlaxcalan allies were killed; Cortés reputedly wept under a tree at dawn.

label: Battle of Otumba (Jul 7, 1520)
sub: Plain northeast of Tenochtitlan where a massive Aztec army intercepted the fleeing Spanish and allied force on July 7, 1520. Cortés's cavalry charge targeting the Aztec commander (and capturing the royal standard) caused the Aztec army to break off pursuit, saving the conquest.

label: Battle of Tucapel (1553)
sub: Site in southern Chile where Mapuche forces under Lautaro ambushed and destroyed Governor Pedro de Valdivia's force on Christmas Day 1553. Valdivia was captured and executed — according to tradition by having molten gold poured down his throat. The battle launched the great Mapuche uprising.

label: Santa Fe (Pueblo Revolt, 1680)
sub: Capital of New Mexico, seized by Pueblo people under Popé on August 21, 1680, driving the Spanish governor and 2,000 settlers south to El Paso. The Pueblo Revolt is the most successful indigenous uprising against Spanish colonial rule in North America; Spain reconquered New Mexico only in 1692.

label: Baracoa (Hatuey Burned, 1512)
sub: Eastern Cuban town near where Taíno chief Hatuey was burned at the stake in February 1512 after leading guerrilla resistance against Diego Velázquez's conquest of Cuba. Hatuey became the first known martyr of indigenous resistance in the Americas. Bartolomé de las Casas witnessed the execution.

label: Templo Mayor (Toxcatl Massacre, 1520)
sub: Sacred precinct of Tenochtitlan where Pedro de Alvarado's forces massacred hundreds of unarmed Aztec nobles and warriors during the Toxcatl festival (May 1520), igniting the general uprising that resulted in La Noche Triste. The Templo Mayor's ruins lie beneath Mexico City's historic center.

label: Jalisco (Mixtón War, 1541–42)
sub: Region of western Mexico where the Caxcán and allied peoples launched the Mixtón War (1541–42), seizing fortified hilltops and defeating several Spanish forces before Viceroy Mendoza personally led a massive campaign to suppress the revolt. The region later became the center of the Chichimec War.

label: Mexico City (Capital of New Spain)
sub: Built directly on the ruins of Tenochtitlan beginning in 1521–22. Capital of the Viceroyalty of New Spain, the most populous and wealthiest Spanish colony. The Cathedral Metropolitan was built over the Templo Mayor. By 1650 it was the largest city in the Americas with ~100,000 inhabitants.

label: Lima (Capital of Peru)
sub: Founded by Francisco Pizarro on January 18, 1535, as Ciudad de los Reyes (City of Kings). Capital of the Viceroyalty of Peru. Major center of colonial administration, trade, and the Inquisition. Home of San Marcos University (1551), the first university in the Americas.

label: Santo Domingo (First Colonial Capital)
sub: Founded by Bartholomew Columbus in 1496 (rebuilt 1502 by Ovando), the oldest continuously occupied European settlement in the Americas and first capital of the Spanish colonial empire. It served as the base for all subsequent Caribbean and mainland conquests.

label: Havana (Cuba Colonial Capital)
sub: Founded 1514, became Cuba's capital in 1553 and the main transit port for the Spanish silver fleet (flota). Havana's harbor was the last port of call before the Atlantic crossing to Spain. Heavily fortified against pirates and foreign powers. Cuba remained Spanish until 1898.

label: Cartagena de Indias
sub: Founded 1533, became the main port for the New Kingdom of Granada (present-day Colombia) and a major slave trading port. Its massive fortifications (including El Castillo San Felipe) were constructed to defend against pirate attacks — including Francis Drake, who sacked the city in 1586.

label: Bogotá (New Kingdom of Granada)
sub: Founded by Gonzalo Jiménez de Quesada in 1538 on the site of the Muisca confederation's territories. Capital of the New Kingdom of Granada, incorporating present-day Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, and Panama. The Muisca were the source of the El Dorado legend.

label: Santiago de Chile
sub: Founded by Pedro de Valdivia on February 12, 1541, in the Mapocho Valley. Capital of the Captaincy General of Chile. Nearly destroyed by Mapuche attack in September 1541. The city was at the frontier of the Araucanian War and the southernmost major Spanish settlement for much of the colonial era.

label: Buenos Aires (River Plate)
sub: First founded by Pedro de Mendoza in 1536, abandoned due to indigenous resistance, and permanently re-established in 1580. Capital of the Río de la Plata region. Its importance grew with Bourbon reforms (1776) when it became capital of a new Viceroyalty of Río de la Plata.

label: Potosí (Silver Capital of the World)
sub: Silver mining city founded 1545 at Cerro Rico ('Rich Mountain') at 4,100 meters altitude in present-day Bolivia. Produced ~60% of the world's silver 1545–1800. By 1650 had 160,000 inhabitants — larger than London or Paris. An estimated 8 million workers died in its mines over 250 years.

label: Mérida (Capital of Yucatán)
sub: Founded by Francisco de Montejo the Younger in 1542 on the ruins of the Maya city T'hó. Capital of the Yucatan conquest. The Cathedral of Mérida (1598) was built with stones from demolished Maya temples. Center of the prolonged Maya resistance throughout the colonial period.

label: Asunción (Paraguay)
sub: Founded 1537 as a Guaraní-Spanish settlement, becoming one of the most significant early inland colonies. Center of the Jesuit Reductions (1609–1767), a controversial but relatively humane experiment in indigenous missionary organization that sheltered 150,000 Guaraní at its peak.

label: Lisbon (Portuguese Imperial Capital)
sub: Capital of the Portuguese maritime empire, the world's first global empire. Center of the spice trade and African slave trade. The Treaty of Tordesillas (1494) divided the world between Portugal and Spain, and Lisbon managed its share: Brazil, Africa, and the route to Asia.

label: Porto Seguro (Cabral's Brazilian Landfall)
sub: Site on the Bahia coast where Pedro Álvares Cabral made landfall on April 22, 1500, claiming Brazil for Portugal. Pero Vaz de Caminha wrote the first detailed European description of Brazil and its indigenous people (Tupi). Portugal initially showed little interest beyond dyewood trade.

label: Salvador da Bahia (First Brazilian Capital)
sub: Founded 1549 as the first capital of Brazil and administrative center of the Portuguese colony. Center of the Atlantic slave trade — more enslaved Africans passed through Salvador than any other port in the Americas. Governed by Mem de Sá who led devastating campaigns against indigenous Tupi peoples.

label: Recife / Pernambuco (Sugar Colony)
sub: Center of Brazil's sugar plantation economy from the 1530s. Pernambuco was the world's largest sugar producer by 1600. The region imported massive numbers of enslaved Africans. Briefly occupied by the Dutch (1630–1654) during the colonial period.

label: São Paulo (Bandeirante Base)
sub: Founded by Jesuit missionaries in 1554. Base for the bandeirantes — Portuguese and mestizo slave raiders who penetrated deep into South America hunting indigenous people for enslavement and searching for gold and silver. Their raids destroyed Jesuit missions and pushed Spain's frontier westward.

label: Rio de Janeiro
sub: Founded 1565 to expel French Huguenot settlers who had established a colony (France Antarctique) in Guanabara Bay. Became Brazil's capital in 1763. Major port for the slave trade and gold exports. Home of the largest urban enslaved African population in the Americas by the 18th century.

label: São Luís (Maranhão)
sub: Founded by French colonists in 1612 and seized by Portugal in 1615. Las Casas-inspired campaigns against indigenous enslavement were repeatedly suppressed by colonists demanding labor. The Jesuit António Vieira preached here against indigenous slavery in the 1650s.

label: Hispaniola (Taíno Heartland)
sub: The island shared by Haiti and the Dominican Republic, home to the Taíno chiefdom of Xaraguá under Anacaona. Site of the first sustained European colonization and the first indigenous genocide in the Americas: Taíno population estimated at 400,000+ in 1492, effectively extinct within 50 years.

label: Sacsayhuamán (Inca Fortress)
sub: Massive Inca fortress above Cusco, built with precisely fitted stones weighing up to 125 tons. Key site during Manco Inca's 1536 rebellion — Inca forces held Sacsayhuamán against the Spanish, who eventually seized it at the cost of many lives including indigenous leader Juan Calvito.

label: Palmares (Maroon State)
sub: Location of the Quilombo dos Palmares, a maroon state of escaped enslaved Africans in northeastern Brazil (c.1605–1694). At its peak sheltered 20,000–30,000 people under leader Zumbi dos Palmares. Resisted numerous Portuguese military expeditions before being destroyed in 1694.

label: St. Augustine, Florida (1565)
sub: Founded by Pedro Menéndez de Avilés on September 8, 1565, the oldest continuously occupied European settlement in the continental United States. Established to counter French Protestant settlements in Florida. Center of Spain's mission system to Florida's indigenous peoples.

label: Amazon (Orellana Expedition, 1541–42)
sub: Route of Francisco de Orellana's 1541–42 navigation of the entire Amazon River, becoming the first European to traverse it. Orellana described densely populated cities along the Amazon — dismissed as fantasy for centuries but confirmed by 21st-century archaeology as accounts of the 'garden cities' of Amazonia.

label: Grand Canyon (First European View, 1540)
sub: A detachment of Coronado's expedition under García López de Cárdenas became the first Europeans to see the Grand Canyon in 1540. The expedition explored the American Southwest in search of the mythical Seven Cities of Cíbola.

label: Mississippi River (De Soto Crossing, 1541)
sub: Hernando de Soto became the first European to cross the Mississippi River in May 1541. He died of fever on its banks in 1542. His expedition introduced disease that devastated Mississippian cultures; many of the large towns he described had vanished by the time the next Europeans arrived.

label: Acapulco (Manila Galleon Port)
sub: Mexican Pacific port that served as the eastern terminus of the Manila Galleon trade route (1565–1815). Each year, one or two galleons arrived from Manila laden with Asian goods (silk, porcelain, spices), returning with American silver. The trade route created the first global economy.

label: Cusco Plaza (Executions of Cuauhtémoc / Tupac Amaru)
sub: Cusco's main plaza (Huacaypata) was the site of the public execution of Tupac Amaru I (September 24, 1572) and Tupac Amaru II's wife and children (1781). The plaza was the symbolic center of the Inca empire; making it a site of colonial violence was a deliberate act of domination.

label: Tenochtitlan Causeways (75-Day Siege, 1521)
sub: The three main causeways connecting Tenochtitlan to the mainland — Tepeyac, Tlacopan, and Iztapalapa — were the axes of the 75-day siege of Tenochtitlan (May 22–August 13, 1521). Spanish brigantines on Lake Texcoco cut off supplies while allied forces advanced street by street.

label: Huánuco Pampa (Inca Administrative Center)
sub: Major Inca administrative center on the Royal Road (Qhapaq Ñan), containing a central plaza large enough to hold 35,000 people for ceremonies and an estimated 500 storehouses for redistributing food across the empire. Exemplifies Inca statecraft through control of storage and redistribution.

label: La Paz (Bolivia)
sub: Founded 1548 by Alonso de Mendoza in the heart of Aymara territory on the Altiplano. Served as an administrative center on the Potosí trade route. Tupac Amaru II's rebellion reached La Paz in 1781 when indigenous forces under Tupac Katari besieged the city for months.

label: Caracas (Venezuela)
sub: Founded 1567 as Santiago de León de Caracas in the valley of the Caracas indigenous people. Capital of the Province of Venezuela and later the Captaincy General of Venezuela (1777). Simón Bolívar, the liberator of South America, was born here in 1783.

label: Muisca Confederation (El Dorado Origin)
sub: Homeland of the Muisca people of the Colombian highlands, source of the El Dorado legend. The Muisca conducted ceremonies at Lake Guatavita in which a new chief (El Dorado, 'the gilded one') was covered in gold dust. Gonzalo Jiménez de Quesada conquered the Muisca in 1537–38.

label: Lake Guatavita (El Dorado Ceremony)
sub: Sacred lake of the Muisca people where the 'gilded chief' (El Dorado) ceremony was performed: a new chief was covered in gold dust and made offerings of gold to the lake's deity. The ceremony gave rise to the El Dorado myth that drove dozens of disastrous exploration expeditions deep into South America.

label: San Juan, Puerto Rico
sub: Settled by Juan Ponce de León in 1508–09; San Juan founded 1521 on its current peninsula. First European settlement in Puerto Rico. Ponce de León was the first governor. Puerto Rico was the staging point for his 1513 Florida expedition. The island remained Spanish until the Spanish-American War of 1898.

label: Cahokia Mounds (Mississippian)
sub: Largest pre-Columbian city north of Mexico, peak population 10,000–20,000 around 1100 CE. Monks Mound at its center is larger at its base than the Great Pyramid of Giza. By the time de Soto's expedition passed through the Mississippi Valley (1539–42), Cahokia had long been abandoned but Mississippian culture persisted in dozens of chiefdoms.

label: Jaragua (Anacaona Massacre, 1503)
sub: Region of southwestern Hispaniola ruled by Taíno cacica (chieftess) Anacaona. Governor Ovando invited Anacaona and 80 subordinate caciques to a feast in 1503, then burned the building with them inside and hanged Anacaona publicly — eliminating organized Taíno leadership in one treacherous act.

label: Cozumel Island (Cortés First Stop, 1519)
sub: First landing of Cortés's 1519 expedition after Cuba. At Cozumel, Cortés rescued Spanish castaway Jerónimo de Aguilar (shipwrecked 1511), who spoke Maya and became a vital interpreter alongside Malintzin. Cortés also destroyed Maya idols and installed Christian images — establishing his pattern of forced religious conversion.

label: Huancavelica (Mercury Mines)
sub: Site of the world's largest mercury deposits, exploited from 1564 to supply mercury for the silver amalgamation process at Potosí. Huancavelica's mines were almost as deadly as Potosí — mercury poisoning caused hidrasgismo (mercury tremors), tooth loss, and death. The mines powered Potosí's silver production but at a terrible human cost to Andean mita workers.

label: Tayasal (Last Independent Maya State)
sub: Island capital of the Itza Maya in Lake Petén Itzá, Guatemala — the last independent Maya state. Cortés visited in 1525 and left a horse there (it died and was later worshipped as a god). The Spanish did not conquer Tayasal until 1697, 205 years after Columbus's first voyage.
